items.q: What happens to an enzyme when it is denatured?
items.correct: Its tertiary structure (3D shape) changes, particularly the active site, so it can no longer bind substrate effectively.
items.misconception: The enzyme has died because enzymes are alive.
items.random_wrong: The enzyme has been completely broken into amino acids.
items.why: Students think of enzymes as alive (probably because they are produced by living things), so they describe denaturation as death rather than shape change.

items.q: Why are enzymes specific to certain substrates?
items.correct: Only substrates with complementary shape and chemistry to the active site can bind effectively.
items.misconception: Each enzyme can react with any substrate, but works fastest with one type.
items.random_wrong: Enzymes choose their substrate based on which one is most common in the cell.
items.why: Students underestimate how strict the lock-and-key/induced-fit relationship is; they think specificity is a preference rather than a structural requirement.

items.correct: No — humans and chimpanzees share a common ancestor; both have evolved from it.
items.misconception: Humans evolved directly from chimpanzees, which is why chimps still exist as a less-evolved version.
items.random_wrong: Humans evolved from monkeys but chimps evolved from gorillas.
items.why: The phrase 'descended from apes' and linear evolution diagrams encourage 'A turned into B' thinking, missing the branching tree model.

items.q: Is an enzyme used up or 'killed' during the reaction it catalyses?
items.correct: No — an enzyme is a catalyst; it is not used up and can be reused many times.
items.misconception: Enzymes are used up in the reaction, like a fuel.
items.random_wrong: Enzymes are alive and die after one reaction.
items.why: Students treat enzymes as reactants or living things, but they are reusable protein catalysts that lower activation energy without being consumed.

items.q: Does cooling an enzyme below its optimum permanently destroy it?
items.correct: No — cold slows an enzyme down but does not denature it; warming it back up restores activity.
items.misconception: Low temperature denatures an enzyme just like high temperature.
items.random_wrong: Enzymes work fastest at 0°C because they are best when cold.
items.why: Only high temperature (and extreme pH) permanently changes the active site; cold merely reduces collisions, so the effect is reversible.

items.q: An enzyme stops working above its optimum temperature because:
items.why: Correct answer: its active site changes shape (denatures). “it dissolves” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which is the best optimum pH for pepsin (in stomach)?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: pH 2. “pH 7” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: If an enzyme reaction takes 20 s at 25°C and 5 s at 35°C, how many times faster at 35°C?
items.why: The real figure is 4 x. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
